planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost danger in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; garden balsam thrives in the heat and humidity of Southern summers and is one of the most reliable and colorful warm-season annuals for shade and partial-shade gardens in zones 8–11.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface or barely covered in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade to part sun; thin to 10–18 inches. Impatiens balsamina (garden balsam) is an old-fashioned cottage-garden annual — among the first flowering plants brought from Asia to Europe in the 16th century — producing upright, fleshy stems densely clothed with double, camellia-like flowers 1–2 inches across tucked along the stem in pink, red, white, lilac, salmon, and bicolor. The mature seed pods explode and scatter seeds with light touch (giving rise to 'touch-me-not' and the genus name Impatiens), and plants self-seed freely in warm, moist gardens. Garden balsam is one of the best heat-tolerant, humid-climate alternatives to the common impatiens (I. walleriana, which succumbs to impatiens downy mildew in humid areas) — it thrives through Southern summers with vigor and color when many annual flowers struggle. The compact, double-flowered 'Tom Thumb' series is popular; the taller 'Camellia-flowered' types are favored for cutting.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–October in zone 9) for winter and spring bloom, or in earliest spring; Moroccan toadflax is a cool-season annual that must bloom before summer heat arrives — it performs best sown in fall in mild-winter climates and in very early spring in colder regions.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in fall or very early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; press in lightly and keep moist — seeds need light to germinate. Linaria maroccana (Moroccan toadflax) is a slender, delicate, cool-season annual producing miniature snapdragon-like flowers just 1/2 to 3/4 inch long with a small spur on dense, upright spikes in an exceptional range of colors — violet, pink, red, orange, yellow, white, and bicolor — all with a yellow or orange eye that gives the flowers a jewel-like quality at close range. The extremely fine, thread-like foliage is nearly invisible under the mass of flowers. 'Fairy Bouquet' is the classic mixed-color strain; 'Northern Lights' has larger flowers; 'Fantasy' is dwarf (8–9 inches). Moroccan toadflax self-seeds freely in suitable gardens and can naturalize in rock gardens and gravel paths. In zone 9, fall-sown plants bloom from December through March — providing cool-season color when few annuals are in bloom. It is outstanding combined with poppies, larkspur, and bachelor buttons in cool-season cutting gardens.

common_name: African Daisy (Freeway Daisy, Trailing African Daisy)

planting.note: Plant container transplants in spring or fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; trailing African daisy is heat- and drought-tolerant once established and is one of the most successful perennial groundcovers for sunny, dry slopes and roadside banks in California and the Gulf South.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; trailing African daisy establishes best in fall or early spring and becomes drought-tolerant once rooted. Osteospermum fruticosum (trailing African daisy or freeway daisy) is a sprawling, mat-forming perennial producing cheerful white, lavender, or purple ray flowers (1.5–2 inches across) with blue-purple disk centers in enormous profusion from fall through spring, with some blooms year-round in mild climates; blooming peaks in late winter and spring when the mat literally disappears under flowers. This South African native was heavily planted along California freeways beginning in the 1960s, giving rise to its common name 'freeway daisy'; it is now widely naturalized in coastal California. In zones 9–11, it performs as a perennial groundcover for hot, sunny, dry slopes, road banks, and xeric garden borders where its trailing stems quickly form a weed-suppressing mat. It does not tolerate frost below about 25°F but rebounds rapidly from light damage. Shear or mow hard after the main bloom flush to encourage compact regrowth and repeat bloom.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Groundcovers
Arizona State University — Desert Plants Database

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, or transplant purchased bedding plants after frost danger; in zones 9–11, Gazania rigens grows as a perennial groundcover that blooms most heavily in spring and fall, blooming lightly through summer heat but persisting year-round.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting or direct sow after frost in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; gazanias perform best and bloom most profusely in hot, dry conditions. Gazania rigens (treasure flower) is a South African native that produces brilliantly colored, 2-to-3-inch daisy flowers with a distinctive, contrasting dark eye ring and ornately patterned petals — in colors ranging from brilliant orange, yellow, and red to pink, white, and multicolor bicolor combinations — on compact, rosette-forming plants with silvery-gray, hairy, lobed leaves. The flowers close in shade, on cloudy days, and at night (a characteristic of the genus); they open fully only in bright sunshine, making them most spectacular on warm, sunny days. Gazanias are outstanding in hot, dry, infertile conditions (poor sandy soil, coastal exposure, reflected heat from pavement) where most annuals fail. In zones 9–11, they may persist as tender perennials; elsewhere treat as summer annuals. The 'Daybreak' series has early, reliable blooms that stay open somewhat longer than older types.

common_name: Brazilian Joseph Coat (Purple Calico Plant, Bloodleaf Joyweed)

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or purchased transplants after last frost in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Brazilian joyweed roots easily from cuttings placed in moist soil or water, making propagation trivially simple for overwintering or expanding plantings.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun (for deepest foliage color) to part shade; tip-prune regularly to maintain bushy, compact growth and prevent legginess. Alternanthera brasiliana (Brazilian Joseph coat) is a fast-growing tropical tender perennial or warm-season annual grown for its spectacular deep-purple to burgundy-purple, bronze, and maroon foliage — among the most richly colored of all foliage annuals. The narrow, lance-shaped to spatula-shaped leaves are a uniform deep burgundy-purple above and lighter purple below; the color is most intense in full sun. Like other alternantheras, it is used extensively as an edging or carpet-bedding plant in formal annual color schemes, and its rich foliage color creates outstanding contrast against chartreuse, gold, or white flowering annuals. Brazilian joyweed grows rapidly in warm, humid conditions and provides consistent foliage color from spring through frost without the need for regular deadheading. In zone 9–10, it often survives mild winters and resprouts from roots; take stem cuttings in fall to overwinter indoors in colder zones.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers
Clemson Cooperative Extension — Annual Bedding Plants
